text: A 77-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer is irritable and asks for morphine exactly on time. Most likely explanation?
rationale: Dx: Undertreated pain. Irritability in cancer patients often suggests inadequate pain control rather than addiction.

text: A 21-year-old man presents with status epilepticus (seizure >5 mins). What is the first-line pharmacotherapy?
rationale: Dx: Status Epilepticus (GCSE). Tx: Benzodiazepines (e.g. lorazepam) are first-line to terminate activity.

text: A 4-year-old boy has fever, sore throat, neck stiffness, and trismus (unable to open mouth). Likely diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Retropharyngeal abscess (RPA). Common in children 2-4 after a URI; presents with trismus.

text: A 56-year-old IV drug user has palpable purpura, arthralgia, and positive cryoglobulins. Next best test?
rationale: Dx: Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis. 90% are associated with Hepatitis C infection.

text: A woman taking lithium for bipolar disorder is pregnant. What fetal anomaly is most likely associated?
rationale: Risk: Lithium in pregnancy increases the risk of Ebstein anomaly (cardiac defect).

text: A 25-year-old female fears embarrassment or scrutiny at office parties and meeting new people. Diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Social anxiety disorder (Social phobia).

text: Regarding social anxiety disorder, what is the first-line treatment?
rationale: Tx: CBT is first-line; Second-line includes SSRIs or B-blockers.

text: A patient on prochlorperazine feels restless and cannot stop moving his legs. Which medication prevents this?
rationale: Dx: Akathisia. Tx: Prevention is maintained by using benzodiazepines like lorazepam.

text: 72yo with carotid bruit; Duplex shows complete occlusion of right ICA. Asymptomatic. Best management?
rationale: Tx: Medical therapy only. No benefit for CEA in complete occlusion.

text: Which HTN medication has the highest risk of developing diabetes mellitus?
rationale: Risk: Thiazide diuretics (Chlorthalidone) increase glucose levels most.

text: Diagnostic tests have higher Positive Predictive Value (PPV) than screening tests primarily because of:
rationale: Dx: PPV increases with higher disease prevalence in the tested population.

text: A 34-year-old smoker has family history of RA. Which likely reduces her risk of RA?
rationale: Risk: Smoking is a provocative factor for seropositive RA.

text: 58yo man, 20yr GERD history, smoker, obese. symptoms controlled on omeprazole. Appropriate management?
rationale: Dx: Screen for Barrett esophagus via endoscopy in men >50 with >5yr GERD.

text: Which symptom helps distinguish PTSD from Acute Stress Disorder?
rationale: Dx: PTSD requires a duration of symptoms >1 month.

text: 67yo man, knee 'pop', inferiorly displaced patella (patella baja), unable to extend knee. Diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Quadriceps tendon rupture causes patella baja.

text: Collapse after laughter, 4mo daytime sleepiness, vivid dreams upon falling asleep. Diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Narcolepsy. Characterized by cataplexy (muscle tone loss with emotion).

text: Best next step in confirming the diagnosis for suspected narcolepsy?
rationale: Dx: Polysomnography is the diagnostic study of choice.

text: First-line pharmacotherapy for daytime somnolence in narcolepsy?
rationale: Tx: Modafinil is first-line for somnolence in narcolepsy.

text: 2yo boy has large amount of painless 'brick-red' rectal bleeding. Abdomen is normal. Diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Meckel diverticulum is the most common malformation for painless rectal bleeding in toddlers.

text: How long must a patient remain seizure-free before resuming driving in Canada?
rationale: Tx: Patients must typically be seizure-free for 6-12 months before driving.

text: A nurse with hyperglycemia (HbA1c 14%) on insulin continues to have high sugars. Which test helps diagnose?
rationale: Dx: C-peptide distinguishes factitious insulin use from endogenous production.

text: Cirrhosis with hematemesis. What is the most appropriate next step?
rationale: Tx: Therapeutic endoscopy is the gold standard for managing variceal bleeding.

text: Child treated for anaphylaxis in ER. What advice should be given at discharge?
rationale: Tx: Education on self-administration of Epinephrine is mandatory.

text: Antidote for Acetaminophen overdose with markedly elevated liver enzymes?
rationale: Tx: NAC is the antidote of choice for hepatotoxicity.

text: Sore throat followed 3 weeks later by hematuria, high BP, and RBC casts. Lab test?
rationale: Dx: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. ASO titer helps confirm recent infection.

text: 5yo with diarrhea and abdominal distention (thin child). What to avoid?
rationale: Dx: Celiac disease. Tx: Strict gluten-free diet.

text: Explosion victim, face burns, soot on nostrils and mouth. Next step?
rationale: Risk: Imminent airway obstruction. Tx: Early intubation is life-saving.

text: Hypopigmented patches on the trunk. Organism?
rationale: Dx: Tinea versicolor.

text: Localized hair loss in scalp with 'broken hair' sign. Likely diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Tinea capitis or Trichotillomania.

text: Dryness on skin with itching. Treatment?
rationale: Tx: Use emollients first-line for xerosis.

text: Itchy papules in finger webs, family members affected. Diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Scabies.
